measurements.measurement_reference: Ground floor door opening ~2.1m tall, approximately 28 courses from threshold to lintel. Ground floor window ~1.3m, approximately 18 courses.

brick_analysis.bond_notes: Standard half-lap running bond throughout brick portion of facade. Upper storey is clad in vertical siding (not brick). Brick is limited to ground floor and part of second floor.

colour.description: Bright red-orange machine-pressed brick. Relatively uniform colour — a warmer, more vivid red than the darker red-brown typical of older Kensington houses. This suggests a later construction date (1910s-1920s) or higher-quality brick. Clean smooth face with minimal texture.

special_features.description: Brick rowlock course forming projecting window sills

special_features.location: above ground floor windows and door
special_features.description: Simple flat brick lintels above openings

condition.notes: A relatively simple 2-storey residential building with brick on the lower portion and vertical siding above. The brick is in good condition with intact mortar. A large ground-floor storefront window suggests this may have been a shop/commercial use at some point. The bright red-orange brick colour is distinctive and somewhat newer-looking than surrounding heritage buildings.

texture_calibration.notes: Door height provides good vertical scale reference. Photo is rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise from upright. Large storefront window and door provide multiple cross-references for measurement. Upper portion is siding — brick model only needed for lower facade.
